content.text: Completion prompts treat the model as a document continuation engine: you provide the beginning of a text and the model fills in what comes next. This was the dominant pattern for GPT-3 and other base models before instruction-tuning became standard. The model does not receive a command — it receives a partial document and continues it according to its training distribution. While instruction prompts are now more common in chat APIs, completion prompts remain essential for base model APIs, for prefix forcing in chat models, and for any task where controlling the exact start of the output matters more than giving the model latitude to choose its own opening.
Completion vs Instruction Framing
Dimension
Completion prompt
Instruction prompt
Model action
Continues the text
Executes the command
Works best with
Base / pretrained models
Instruction-tuned / chat models
Output start
Controlled by the prompt's last token
Model chooses freely
Preamble risk
None — output begins immediately
Model may add "Sure! Here is..."
Steering mechanism
Partial document structure and style
Explicit constraints in the command
Completion Prompt Patterns

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()


def complete(prefix: str, max_tokens: int = 150, stop: list[str] | None = None) -> str:
    """
    Simulates a completion prompt using the chat API by prefilling
    the assistant turn — the model continues from exactly where you left off.
    """
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=max_tokens,
        temperature=0.3,
        messages=[
            {"role": "user", "content": "Continue the text."},
            {"role": "assistant", "content": prefix},   # prefill forces the start
        ],
        stop_sequences=stop or [],
    )
    # The response continues FROM prefix — prepend to get the full output
    return prefix + resp.content[0].text


# Pattern 1: Document continuation
print(complete(
    "The three laws of thermodynamics state that:",
    max_tokens=120,
))


# Pattern 2: Code completion (natural-feeling — partial function body)
print(complete(
    """def is_palindrome(s: str) -> bool:
    \"\"\"Return True if s reads the same forwards and backwards.\"\"\"
    """,
    max_tokens=60,
    stop=["\n\n"],
))


# Pattern 3: Structured data completion
print(complete(
    '{"name": "Alice", "age": 30, "city": "',
    max_tokens=20,
    stop=['"'],   # stop at closing quote to get just the value
))


# Pattern 4: Dialogue completion (few-shot style)
print(complete(
    """Q: What is the capital of Japan?
A: Tokyo.

Q: What is the capital of Australia?
A: Canberra.

Q: What is the capital of Brazil?
A:""",
    max_tokens=10,
    stop=["\n"],
))
Using Stop Sequences to Bound the Output

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()


def extract_label(text: str, labels: list[str]) -> str:
    """
    Completion prompt that forces a single-word answer by stopping
    at the first newline. The partial prefix guides the model to pick
    from the label set without needing an instruction.
    """
    label_list = ", ".join(labels)
    prefix = f"""Text: {text}
Sentiment ({label_list}):"""

    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=10,
        temperature=0.0,
        stop_sequences=["\n", "."],
        messages=[
            {"role": "user", "content": "Complete the classification."},
            {"role": "assistant", "content": prefix},
        ],
    )
    return resp.content[0].text.strip()


examples = [
    "The product arrived damaged and customer support was useless.",
    "Absolutely love the build quality — exceeded my expectations.",
    "It works. Nothing special.",
]

for text in examples:
    label = extract_label(text, ["Positive", "Negative", "Neutral"])
    print(f"  {label:10s}  {text[:55]}...")
Prefix Forcing in Chat Models
Chat-format APIs (including Anthropic's Messages API) support a completion-style trick: by providing an
assistant

turn as the last message, you force the model to continue from that exact prefix rather than generating its own opening. This is called prefix forcing and is the cleanest way to eliminate "Sure, here is..." preambles or to ensure the output starts with a specific character such as
for JSON output.

import anthropic
import json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()


def extract_json(text: str) -> dict:
    """
    Use prefix forcing to guarantee the output starts with '{',
    eliminating any preamble before the JSON object.
    """
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=200,
        temperature=0.0,
        messages=[
            {
                "role": "user",
                "content": (
                    f"Extract the following fields from the text and return as JSON:\n"
                    f"fields: name, email, company\n\nText: {text}"
                ),
            },
            # Prefix forces the output to start with '{'
            {"role": "assistant", "content": "{"},
        ],
    )
    # Reconstruct the full JSON: the prefilled '{' + what the model continued
    raw = "{" + resp.content[0].text
    return json.loads(raw)


result = extract_json(
    "Hi, I'm Sarah Connor from Cyberdyne Systems. Reach me at sarah@cyberdyne.com."
)
print(result)
# → {'name': 'Sarah Connor', 'email': 'sarah@cyberdyne.com', 'company': 'Cyberdyne Systems'}

Completion prompts are not obsolete — they encode a fundamentally different relationship with the model. Where instruction prompts delegate the task, completion prompts shape the generation space through document structure. The two techniques are complementary: use instructions to specify what to do, and use prefix forcing (a completion technique applied inside the chat format) to control exactly how the output begins. Mastering both gives you precise control over both task specification and output format.
What Completion Prompts Are

A completion prompt is the oldest form of interaction with a language model: you supply a prefix of text and the model extends it according to the probability distribution it learned during pretraining. The model has no notion of a command or user intent — it simply asks "given all the text to the left, what token is most likely next?" and repeats that process until a stop condition is met. Every language model, regardless of how sophisticated its instruction-following has become, begins its life as a completion engine.

The distinction between completion and instruction prompts is architectural. Base (pretrained) models respond only to completion-style input because they were never fine-tuned to recognise the concept of a user giving a command. Instruction-tuned models were further trained on human demonstrations, making them prefer instruction-style input — but they still support prefix completion through the assistant-prefill mechanism, where the last message in a conversation is an incomplete assistant turn that the model is forced to continue from.
Why Completion Prompts Matter

Instruction prompts are now the default for most developers, but they introduce a layer of indirection: the model must parse your instruction, plan a response, decide how to open it, and then produce the content. That sequence adds variability. When you need the output to begin with an exact token — a JSON brace, a CSV column header, a specific word — instruction prompts frequently produce preambles like "Here is the JSON you requested:" that break downstream parsers.

Completion prompts eliminate this ambiguity entirely. By providing the opening of the output yourself, you guarantee the model cannot add prefatory text because it is already mid-document. This is critical in production pipelines where structured outputs are consumed by code, not humans. A single stray character before the opening brace of a JSON object crashes a parser. Completion-style prefix forcing is the most reliable defence against that failure mode.

For base models accessed through APIs that do not support a chat format — older GPT-3 endpoints, Llama served via text completion APIs, or custom deployments — completion prompting is the only available interaction mode. Even for teams using modern chat APIs, understanding completion prompting is essential because it is the mental model underlying every other prompting technique: few-shot examples, chain-of-thought, role prompts, and format constraints all work by shaping the probability distribution the model draws from when predicting the next token.
Benefits of Completion Prompting
Deterministic output start:
The output begins exactly where your prefix ends. No preamble, no hedging, no "certainly!".
Format enforcement:
Prefilling with '{' or '[' forces JSON/array output without instruction-time negotiation.
Works on base models:
Compatible with any language model regardless of instruction-tuning status.
Natural few-shot integration:
Input–output demonstrations fit naturally as part of the document being continued.
Lower latency for fixed-format tasks:
Fewer tokens wasted on preamble means first meaningful token arrives sooner.
Transparent control:
Every character of the output start is yours; there is no ambiguity about what the model was "told" to do.
Trade-offs and Limitations

Completion prompts are not universally better than instruction prompts. Their primary weakness is that they require you to know exactly what the output should look like before you ask for it. If you want the model to make open-ended decisions about structure, tone, or content, a completion prompt forces you to leave those elements underspecified in the prefix — which the model will fill in based on training distribution rather than your unstated preferences.
Requires output knowledge up front:
You must know the output format before writing the prefix. Instruction prompts let you describe the format in natural language.
Harder to iterate:
Adjusting a completion prompt means rewriting the prefix; adjusting an instruction prompt is more natural.
Stop sequence brittleness:
If your stop sequence appears in the valid output (e.g., a newline inside a JSON string), the completion terminates prematurely.
Not supported by all APIs:
Some chat APIs do not allow an assistant prefill message; you must use instruction prompts instead.
Alignment-tuned models may resist:
Heavily RLHF-tuned models sometimes override a completion prefix if it would lead to policy-violating completions.
How Completion Prompting Works

Every language model is trained to minimise the negative log-likelihood of the training corpus. At each position, the model computes a probability distribution over all vocabulary tokens. Completion prompting exploits this directly: your prefix is tokenised and fed through the model to produce a hidden state, and the model samples the next token from the resulting distribution. That token is appended to the context and the process repeats.
where
h_t
is the final-layer hidden state at position
t
W_U
is the unembedding matrix (the language model head), and
T
is the temperature parameter. Setting
collapses the distribution to a single peak (greedy decoding). Values of
flatten the distribution, increasing diversity at the cost of coherence.

The prefix acts as a hard conditioning signal: every token in your prefix is attended to with full weight by every subsequent position in the generated output. The model cannot ignore or reinterpret the prefix — it must produce text that is consistent with it. This is why prefix forcing is so effective for format control: if you begin with '{', the only continuation with non-negligible probability is a valid JSON structure, because anything else would be inconsistent with patterns in the training data.

Stop sequences work as a post-processing filter on the token stream: the API monitors each decoded token and halts generation as soon as the most recent tokens form the stop string. This is distinct from controlling the model's own probability distribution — stop sequences are enforced externally. Consequently, they can only prevent tokens from being returned; they cannot bias the model to avoid the stop string in longer generations.
When to Use Completion Prompts
Use completion prompts when you need absolute control over the start of the output, when you are working with base models, or when the task is naturally framed as document continuation.
Structured output extraction:
Prefilling '{' or '```json\n{' guarantees JSON output without a parser-breaking preamble.
Classification with forced labels:
End the prefix with 'Sentiment: ' and the model continues with the label word, which you read before any stop sequence fires.
Code completion:
Supply a partial function signature and let the model implement the body — the natural form of code editors and fill-in-the-middle models.
Dialogue with specific character voice:
Provide a few lines of the character's prior dialogue and let the model continue in that voice without needing an elaborate system prompt.
Base model APIs:
The only interaction mode available for models accessed via text-completion endpoints.

Do not use completion prompts when the task requires the model to reason about what to produce before producing it (use chain-of-thought with instruction prompts), when you need the model to ask clarifying questions, or when the output structure varies significantly by input.
How to Implement: Step-by-Step
The following walkthrough builds a robust completion-based JSON extractor using prefix forcing with the Anthropic Messages API.
Identify the fixed prefix:
Determine exactly what characters must start every output. For JSON: '{'. For a list: '['. For a code block: '```python\n'.
Build the user message:
Provide all context the model needs to continue intelligently — the source text, the schema, any constraints.
Set the assistant prefill:
Add {"role": "assistant", "content": prefix} as the last message. The API continues from this exact point.
Configure stop sequences:
Add stop sequences to bound the output length (e.g., ['\n\n'] to stop after the first paragraph).
Reconstruct the full output:
Prepend the prefix to the model's response text. The API returns only what the model generated, not the prefill.
Parse and validate:
For JSON, wrap in try/except json.loads(). Log malformed responses for debugging.

import anthropic
import json
from typing import Any

client = anthropic.Anthropic()


def extract_fields(text: str, schema: dict[str, str]) -> dict[str, Any]:
    """
    Use prefix forcing to extract structured fields from unstructured text.
    The assistant prefill guarantees output begins with '{', eliminating preambles.
    """
    field_desc = ", ".join(f'{k} ({v})' for k, v in schema.items())
    user_message = (
        f"Extract the following fields from the text below.\n"
        f"Fields: {field_desc}\n\n"
        f"Text: {text}\n\n"
        f"Return a single JSON object. Use null for missing fields."
    )

    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=300,
        temperature=0.0,
        messages=[
            {"role": "user", "content": user_message},
            # Prefix forcing: output is forced to start with '{'
            {"role": "assistant", "content": "{"},
        ],
    )

    # Reconstruct: the prefilled '{' + model continuation
    raw = "{" + resp.content[0].text
    return json.loads(raw)


# Test
result = extract_fields(
    text="Contact John Smith at john@example.com; he is the VP of Engineering at Acme Corp.",
    schema={"name": "string", "email": "string", "title": "string", "company": "string"},
)
print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))
# {
#   "name": "John Smith",
#   "email": "john@example.com",
#   "title": "VP of Engineering",
#   "company": "Acme Corp"
# }
Real-World Example: Structured Log Parsing Pipeline

A backend service receives heterogeneous log lines from multiple systems. Some are structured JSON, others are free-text. The pipeline must normalise them into a common schema for ingestion into a time-series database. Completion prompting handles the free-text lines reliably without per-log-format rules.

import anthropic
import json
import re

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

LOG_SCHEMA = {
    "timestamp": "ISO-8601 string or null",
    "level": "DEBUG|INFO|WARN|ERROR|null",
    "service": "service name string or null",
    "message": "human-readable message string",
    "error_code": "string or null",
}


def parse_log_line(line: str) -> dict:
    """Parse an unstructured log line into the common schema."""
    field_desc = "; ".join(f"{k}: {v}" for k, v in LOG_SCHEMA.items())
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=200,
        temperature=0.0,
        messages=[
            {
                "role": "user",
                "content": (
                    f"Parse this log line into JSON.\n"
                    f"Fields: {field_desc}\n\n"
                    f"Log: {line}"
                ),
            },
            {"role": "assistant", "content": "{"},
        ],
        stop_sequences=["\n\n"],
    )
    raw = "{" + resp.content[0].text
    # Remove trailing incomplete keys
    raw = re.sub(r',\s*"[^"]*"?\s*$', '', raw.rstrip())
    if not raw.endswith("}"):
        raw += "}"
    return json.loads(raw)


sample_logs = [
    "2024-01-15T10:23:45Z ERROR [payment-service] Transaction declined: ERR_INSUFFICIENT_FUNDS",
    "Jan 15 10:23:46 auth-service WARN Failed login attempt for user=alice@example.com",
    "[INFO] 2024-01-15 10:23:47 database-pool: Connection pool exhausted (pool_size=10)",
]

for log in sample_logs:
    parsed = parse_log_line(log)
    print(json.dumps(parsed, indent=2))
    print("---")
Common Mistakes
Forgetting to prepend the prefix to the response.
The API returns only what the model generated — not the prefill. Concatenate prefix + response.content[0].text before parsing.
Using a stop sequence that appears in valid content.

If your stop is '
' and the JSON contains a newline-escaped string, the completion truncates mid-object. Use '

' or post-process instead.
Not setting temperature=0 for classification tasks.
A non-zero temperature introduces randomness; you may get a valid token that is not the intended label. Set temperature=0 for any task with a fixed correct answer.
Conflating completion prefix with few-shot examples.
Few-shot examples demonstrate the pattern; the completion prefix forces the output start. They serve different purposes and should both be present for classification pipelines.
Applying completion prompts to open-ended tasks.

Asking the model to 'write a blog post' with a completion prefix of 'The key insight is:' works against you — the model will continue that sentence, not write a structured post. Use instruction prompts for open-ended generation.

# WRONG: forgetting to prepend prefix
resp = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001", max_tokens=100,
    messages=[
        {"role": "user", "content": "Extract name as JSON."},
        {"role": "assistant", "content": "{"},
    ],
)
# BUG: resp.content[0].text starts with '"name"' — missing the opening brace
data = json.loads(resp.content[0].text)  # SyntaxError!

# CORRECT
raw = "{" + resp.content[0].text   # prepend the prefill
data = json.loads(raw)              # now parses cleanly
Best Practices
Always set temperature=0 for deterministic tasks
(classification, extraction). Only use non-zero temperature for generative tasks where variety is desirable.
Keep the prefix minimal.
The shorter your forced prefix, the more latitude the model has to fill in sensible content. Prefilling entire JSON keys forces the model to complete values only.
Combine with schema validation.
Parse the output and validate against your schema (jsonschema, Pydantic) before using it. Malformed JSON is rare but not impossible.
Use few-shot examples in the user message.
Three or four input–output examples dramatically reduce extraction errors, especially for edge cases.
Test stop sequences independently.
Generate 20–30 samples and inspect whether the stop sequence fires at the right position every time.
Log token counts.
Completion prompts for extraction tasks are typically very short. If input_tokens is growing unexpectedly, your context assembly has a bug.
Performance and Scalability

Completion prompts are inherently efficient because they produce minimal output. For classification tasks (e.g., sentiment), the output is a single word, which means max_tokens can be set to 5–10, dramatically reducing cost and latency. A sentiment classifier running on claude-haiku with prefix forcing typically completes in 200–400 ms per request and costs roughly 40–60 input tokens plus 1–3 output tokens per classification.

For batch workloads, the Anthropic Batch API accepts up to 10,000 requests per batch at 50% cost reduction with results available within 24 hours. For streaming extraction pipelines where structured data must be processed incrementally, stream the response and parse JSON incrementally using a streaming JSON parser (ijson for Python) rather than waiting for the full response.

Latency scales linearly with output token count. For fixed-length outputs (a label word, a 3-digit number), latency is bounded and predictable. For variable-length outputs (a JSON object of unknown size), set max_tokens conservatively based on your 99th percentile observed output length, and log cases where the model hits the limit.
Tooling and Ecosystem
Most major LLM APIs support some form of completion prompting, though the mechanism varies:
API
Completion support
Mechanism
Anthropic Messages API
Yes
Assistant prefill: last message role='assistant'
OpenAI Completions
Yes (legacy)
prompt= field in /v1/completions endpoint
OpenAI Chat API
Partial
No native prefill; simulate via suffix in user message
Llama.cpp / Ollama
Yes
prompt= field in /api/generate or OpenAI-compatible /completions
HuggingFace generate()
Yes
input_ids= (tokenized prefix); force_words_ids= for constrained decoding
Security and Reliability

Completion prompts interact with safety systems differently from instruction prompts. Because the model is completing a document rather than following a command, it may be more susceptible to prompt injection attacks embedded in the source text. If your completion prefix includes user-supplied content, that content can appear to be part of the model's "reasoning" rather than an external instruction, potentially bypassing safety classifiers that inspect only the instruction portion of a chat turn.

Always sanitise user-supplied text before including it in a completion prefix. In particular, never interpolate raw user input into the assistant prefill — an attacker can end your JSON object prematurely and inject a new instruction as a fake conversation turn. Validate and escape all dynamic content before assembly.

For reliability, always implement a JSON schema validator on the output and retry once on parse failure with temperature=0. Log the raw response string on failure for debugging. Set a maximum retry count (typically 2) to avoid infinite retry loops on inputs the model genuinely cannot handle.
Key Takeaways
Completion prompts treat the model as a document continuation engine — no commands, no instructions, just token prediction.
Prefix forcing (assistant prefill) is the modern way to apply completion prompting inside chat APIs, guaranteeing an exact output start.
Stop sequences bound output length by halting generation on a specific token string; they do not modify the model's internal probability distribution.
Use temperature=0 for classification and extraction; non-zero only when creative variation is desired.
Always prepend the prefill to the response before parsing — the API returns only the model-generated continuation, not the forced prefix.
Never interpolate raw user input into the assistant prefill — this creates a prompt injection attack surface.
Completion prompts and instruction prompts are complementary: use instructions to specify what to do, prefix forcing to control exactly how the output begins.
